title: The balance of payments
summary: Every international transaction a country makes is recorded somewhere in its balance of payments. This lesson builds the structure — the current account (trade in goods and services, primary income, secondary income), the capital account and the financial account — then shows what a current-account deficit or surplus actually means, why the current and financial accounts broadly offset, what causes and follows a persistent deficit, and how the current account is linked to the exchange rate. Because 4.5 is examined on Paper 3, the focus is quantitative: you will calculate account balances and see exactly how our marking engine awards method and accuracy marks.
learningObjectives: Describe the structure of the balance of payments: the current account, the capital account and the financial account, and their components.
Calculate the balance of trade in goods and services and the current-account balance from raw data, showing working.
Explain the meaning of a current-account deficit or surplus, and why the current and financial accounts broadly offset one another.
Analyse the causes and consequences of a persistent current-account deficit and its link to the exchange rate.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: A Country's Statement of Account with the World
simpleExplanation.summary: The balance of payments records every economic transaction between one country's residents and the rest of the world over a period. It is split so that one part measures what the country earns and spends on trade and income (the current account), and another part measures how that gap is financed by buying and selling assets (the financial account).
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a household. The current account is like the monthly budget: wages coming in versus spending on goods, services and interest. If the household spends more than it earns, it runs a deficit — and it must cover that gap somehow: by drawing down savings, selling an asset, or borrowing. Those financing moves are the financial account. For a country, the two sides mirror each other: a current-account deficit is broadly financed by a financial-account surplus (selling assets to, or borrowing from, foreigners).
steps: Sort each international transaction into the current, capital or financial account.
Within the current account, net off goods, services, primary income and secondary income.
A current-account deficit means outflows on those items exceed inflows; a surplus is the reverse.
The current and financial accounts broadly offset — a deficit on one is matched by a surplus on the other, which links directly to the exchange rate.

flashcards.back: A record of all economic transactions between the residents of a country and the rest of the world over a given period. It is divided into the current account, the capital account and the financial account.

The part of the balance of payments recording trade in goods, trade in services, primary income (net income) and secondary income (net current transfers). It is the account most often discussed in the news.

flashcards.front: The four components of the current account
flashcards.back: 1. Balance of trade in goods (visible trade). 2. Balance of trade in services (invisible trade). 3. Primary income / net income (wages, profit, interest, dividends). 4. Secondary income / net current transfers (aid, remittances, with nothing given in return).
The value of a country's exports of goods minus the value of its imports of goods. A single sub-component of the current account — not the whole current account.

Primary income (net income) is earnings from factors of production supplied abroad — wages, profit, interest and dividends. Secondary income (current transfers) is one-way flows with no good, service or asset exchanged in return — foreign aid, grants, workers' remittances.

A small component of the balance of payments recording capital transfers (e.g. debt forgiveness) and the sale or purchase of non-produced, non-financial assets such as patents, copyrights and trademarks.

Records net changes in the ownership of assets: foreign direct investment (FDI), portfolio investment (shares and bonds), other investment (loans, deposits) and changes in official reserve assets held by the central bank.

The value of outflows on the current account (imports of goods and services, income and transfers paid abroad) exceeds the value of inflows. The country is spending more on current transactions with the rest of the world than it is earning.

flashcards.front: Why the current and financial accounts broadly offset
flashcards.back: A current-account deficit must be financed: foreigners are paid, and that money returns as their acquisition of the country's assets (a financial-account surplus). So a current-account deficit is broadly matched by a financial-account surplus, and vice versa.

flashcards.front: Causes of a persistent current-account deficit
flashcards.back: Loss of international competitiveness (high relative prices/costs), a strong exchange rate, high domestic demand pulling in imports, low domestic saving relative to investment, or a heavy reliance on imported raw materials and energy.

flashcards.front: Consequences of a persistent current-account deficit
flashcards.back: Rising external debt and interest/profit outflows, dependence on foreign capital ('hot money' risk), possible downward pressure on the exchange rate, and — if driven by weak competitiveness — a sign of underlying structural problems. It can, however, be sustainable if it funds productive investment.

flashcards.front: Current account and the exchange rate
flashcards.back: Current-account credits (exports) create demand for the domestic currency; debits (imports) create supply of it. Under a floating rate, a persistent deficit tends to put downward pressure on the currency; the resulting depreciation can, over time, improve competitiveness and narrow the deficit.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: current vs financial account
flashcards.back: Investment flows — FDI, shares, bonds, loans — belong in the FINANCIAL account, not the current account. The current account is trade and income. Placing 'foreign investment' in the current account is a classic error.

quickCheck.prompt: A foreign company builds a new car factory in the country (inward FDI). This transaction is recorded in the:
Which of the following is NOT one of the four components of the current account?
A country has a current-account deficit. Ignoring the small capital account and errors, its financial account must therefore be in:
options: Surplus that broadly offsets the deficit
Surplus twice the size of the deficit
quickCheck.answer: Surplus that broadly offsets the deficit

quickCheck.prompt: Remittances sent home by citizens working abroad, received by the country, are recorded under:
A current-account deficit on the balance of trade in goods specifically means that:
options: Exports of goods exceed imports of goods
Imports of goods exceed exports of goods
The financial account is in deficit
quickCheck.answer: Imports of goods exceed exports of goods

faq.q: What is the difference between the current account and the balance of trade?
faq.a: The balance of trade (in goods, and in services) is just one part of the current account. The full current account also includes primary income (net wages, profit, interest and dividends from abroad) and secondary income (net current transfers such as aid and remittances). A country can have a trade surplus but a current-account deficit if, for example, large income payments flow out to foreign investors. Always check which balance a Paper 3 question is asking for.

faq.q: Why do the current and financial accounts 'broadly' offset rather than exactly?
faq.a: In principle the whole balance of payments sums to zero, because every transaction has two sides. In practice, a small capital account and a 'net errors and omissions' item exist to absorb measurement gaps in the data. So the current and financial accounts broadly offset: a current-account deficit is financed by a financial-account surplus of roughly equal size, with the capital account and errors making up any small difference.

faq.q: Is a current-account deficit always bad?
faq.a: No. A deficit means the country is a net importer of goods, services and income on current transactions — but the judgement depends on why. If foreign capital is funding productive investment that raises future export capacity, a deficit can be sustainable. It becomes a concern when it is large, persistent, driven by weak competitiveness, and financed by volatile short-term borrowing that can reverse suddenly, or by running down reserves.

sections.content: The balance of payments is the accounting framework that records a country's economic relationship with the rest of the world. Because Topic 4.5 is assessed on Paper 3, your job is not only to describe the framework but to work with the numbers — calculating the balance of trade and the current-account balance, and reasoning about how the accounts fit together. This lesson builds the structure component by component, then shows precisely how our marking engine awards Paper 3 method and accuracy marks on a worked calculation.
The structure of the balance of payments

The balance of payments records all economic transactions between a country's residents and the rest of the world over a period. Every transaction is either a credit — an inflow of foreign currency, shown with a plus sign, such as an export — or a debit — an outflow, shown with a minus sign, such as an import. The record is split into three accounts, and taken as a whole the balance of payments sums to zero, because each transaction is entered twice with opposite signs.

items: **Current account** — trade in goods, trade in services, primary income and secondary income. This is the account examined most heavily in 4.5.
**Capital account** — a small account for capital transfers (e.g. debt forgiveness) and non-produced, non-financial assets (e.g. patents, trademarks).
**Financial account** — the ownership of assets: foreign direct investment, portfolio investment, other investment and reserve assets.

sections.content: The current account and its four components

The current account is the sum of four sub-balances. Learn the four precisely, because Paper 3 questions frequently give you the components and ask you to build the current-account balance from them — or give you the current-account balance and one or two components and ask you to work backwards.

items: **Balance of trade in goods (visible trade):** exports of goods minus imports of goods. A deficit here means imports of goods exceed exports.
**Balance of trade in services (invisible trade):** exports of services (tourism, finance, shipping) minus imports of services.
**Primary income (net income):** net earnings from factors of production abroad — wages, profit, interest and dividends received minus those paid to non-residents.
**Secondary income (net current transfers):** one-way flows with nothing given in return — foreign aid, grants and workers' remittances, received minus paid.

sections.content: Current account = (Balance of trade in goods) + (Balance of trade in services) + (Primary income) + (Secondary income)
The capital and financial accounts

The capital account is small: it records capital transfers such as debt forgiveness and the transfer of non-produced, non-financial assets like patents and copyrights. The financial account is where the action is. It records the net change in the ownership of assets between the country and the rest of the world, and it is the account that finances the current account.

items: **Foreign direct investment (FDI):** a lasting interest in an enterprise — building a factory, or a controlling stake in a foreign firm.
**Portfolio investment:** buying and selling financial assets such as shares and bonds without a controlling interest.
**Other investment:** loans, bank deposits and trade credit.
**Reserve assets:** the central bank's holdings of foreign currency and gold, which change as it intervenes in currency markets or finances imbalances.

sections.content: The single most common structural error on Paper 3 is putting investment flows in the wrong account. Trade and income sit in the CURRENT account; the buying and selling of assets — FDI, shares, bonds, loans — sits in the FINANCIAL account. If a data table lists 'net FDI' or 'portfolio investment' among your current-account figures, it does not belong there. Read the labels before you add anything up.
What a current-account deficit or surplus means

A current-account surplus means the value of credits (exports of goods and services, income and transfers received) exceeds the value of debits (imports, income and transfers paid abroad): the country is a net earner on current transactions. A current-account deficit is the reverse — the country spends more on current transactions with the rest of the world than it earns, so it is a net borrower or seller of assets to cover the gap. Crucially, 'deficit' here refers to the current account as a whole, not just to goods.
Why the current and financial accounts broadly offset

If a country buys more from abroad than it sells, foreigners end up holding its currency. That currency does not vanish — it returns as foreign acquisition of the country's assets: foreigners buy its bonds, lend to it, or invest directly. That inflow is recorded as a financial-account surplus. So a current-account deficit is broadly financed by a financial-account surplus of roughly equal size, and a current-account surplus by a financial-account deficit (the country acquires assets abroad). The word 'broadly' matters: the small capital account and a net errors and omissions item absorb measurement gaps, so the offset is close but not exact in published data.
Ignoring the small capital account and statistical errors: Current account balance \\( \approx \\) − Financial account balance

sections.question: A country records: exports of goods $300bn, imports of goods $360bn, net trade in services +$40bn, net primary income −$10bn, net secondary income +$5bn. Calculate the balance of trade in goods and the current-account balance. [4]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — Balance of trade in goods.**
Balance of trade in goods = exports of goods − imports of goods
= $300bn − $360bn
= **−$60bn** (a deficit on goods, as imports exceed exports).

**Step 2 — Current-account balance.**
Current account = balance of trade in goods + net trade in services + net primary income + net secondary income
= (−60) + (+40) + (−10) + (+5)
= −60 + 40 − 10 + 5
= **−$25bn**.

**Conclusion:** the balance of trade in goods is a deficit of $60bn, and the current account is a **deficit of $25bn**.

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks [4]:**
This is a Paper 3 quantitative item, marked on the POINTS approach — separate **M (method)** and **A (accuracy)** marks, with working required and follow-through applied.
- **M1 — goods-balance method:** setting up exports of goods − imports of goods (300 − 360). The method mark is earned for the correct operation even before the final figure.
- **A1 — goods-balance accuracy:** the correct value **−$60bn**, with the negative sign / 'deficit' shown. A bare '60' without sign or a positive 60 loses this accuracy mark.
- **M1 — current-account method:** summing all four components, i.e. (−60) + 40 + (−10) + 5, with the goods balance correctly carried in.
- **A1 — current-account accuracy:** the correct value **−$25bn**, identified as a deficit.
- **Follow-through:** if Step 1 were mis-calculated (say −$50bn) but Step 2 correctly summed that wrong figure with the other components, the second method mark and the follow-through accuracy mark are still awarded — you are not penalised twice for one slip, provided the working is shown. This is why writing out the substitution line is worth marks in itself: no working means no method mark and no follow-through.

sections.content: This is exactly what the Practice button does. Write your own full solution to this calculation — showing every substitution line — submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same Paper 3 POINTS scheme, telling you which M and A marks you earned and where follow-through was applied. The habit that earns Paper 3 marks: SHOW the working line, always attach the sign and unit ($bn), and state 'surplus' or 'deficit' in your conclusion.
Causes and consequences of a persistent current-account deficit

A one-off deficit is rarely a worry; a large, persistent deficit deserves analysis. On Paper 3 you may be asked to calculate a deficit and then to comment on it, so keep the causes and consequences ready to deploy on the numbers in front of you.

items: **Causes — loss of competitiveness:** if domestic prices or unit labour costs rise faster than trading partners', exports become dearer and imports cheaper, widening the deficit.
**Causes — a strong exchange rate:** an overvalued currency makes exports expensive and imports cheap.
**Causes — high domestic demand and low saving:** strong consumption and investment pull in imports and mean the country saves less than it invests, funded from abroad.

**Consequences — rising external liabilities:** financing the deficit means selling assets or borrowing, so future primary-income outflows (interest, profit, dividends) rise, which can worsen the current account further.
**Consequences — vulnerability:** reliance on short-term capital ('hot money') is risky, as sudden outflows can force a sharp depreciation or a fall in reserves.
**Consequences — it can be sustainable:** if foreign capital funds productive investment that raises future export capacity, a deficit need not be damaging.

sections.content: The current account and the exchange rate

The two are tightly linked through the foreign-exchange market. Current-account credits — exports of goods and services — create demand for the domestic currency, because foreign buyers must acquire it to pay. Current-account debits — imports — create supply of the domestic currency, as residents sell it to buy foreign currency. Under a floating exchange rate, a persistent current-account deficit tends to put downward pressure on the currency, because the supply of the currency runs ahead of demand. The resulting depreciation makes exports cheaper and imports dearer, which over time can help narrow the deficit — a self-correcting mechanism, though one that works with lags.

sections.question: The country in the earlier example has a current-account deficit of $25bn. Its capital account is +$2bn and net errors and omissions are −$1bn. Calculate the balance on the financial account, and state what it tells you about the flow of capital. [3]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — Use the balance-of-payments identity.** The whole balance of payments sums to zero:
Current account + Capital account + Financial account + Net errors and omissions = 0.

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**
(−25) + (+2) + Financial account + (−1) = 0
−24 + Financial account = 0
Financial account = **+$24bn**.

**Step 3 — Interpret.** The financial account is a surplus of $24bn: there is a net **inflow** of financial capital. Foreigners are, on net, acquiring the country's assets (through FDI, portfolio investment, lending or a run-down of reserves), and this broadly finances the $25bn current-account deficit — confirming that the current and financial accounts broadly offset.

---
**Marking-engine note:** M1 for setting up the identity and substituting; A1 for the correct **+$24bn** with sign; A1 for the interpretation as a net capital inflow that finances the current-account deficit. Follow-through applies if an earlier figure was carried in incorrectly but the identity was applied correctly.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing the current and financial accounts** — putting FDI, shares, bonds or loans in the current account. Investment flows are financial-account items; the current account is trade and income only.

**Treating 'balance of trade' as the whole current account** — the goods balance is one of four components. Forgetting services, primary income or secondary income gives the wrong current-account figure.

**Saying a current-account deficit simply means 'imports > exports'** — that describes the goods (or trade) balance. A current-account deficit means total current-account debits exceed total current-account credits across all four components.
**Dropping signs and units** — on Paper 3, an answer without a minus sign, or without '$bn', or without stating 'surplus'/'deficit', forfeits the accuracy mark even when the arithmetic is right.
**Omitting the working line** — no shown method means no method mark and no follow-through, so a single arithmetic slip can cost every mark instead of one.
**Claiming the accounts offset exactly** — they broadly offset; the small capital account and net errors and omissions absorb the difference in real data.

sections.content: Where this leads

The balance of payments is the bridge between a country's trade (Topic 4.1–4.4) and its exchange rate and macroeconomic policy. Once you can build and interpret the accounts, the next questions follow naturally: how exchange-rate changes feed back into the current account, and whether governments should act on a persistent deficit. Every one of those debates rests on the framework — and the calculation skills — you have built here.
